comments.text: In this new paper of mine I obtain a weak version of the first result: given any function $\omega(n)$ that goes to infinity as $n \to \infty$, there exists a dense set of $x \in (-1,1)$ such that $L_n(x) \geq \frac{2}{\pi} \log n - \omega(n)$ for infinitely many $n$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Erdős and Vértesi in On the almost everywhere divergence of Lagrange interpolatory polynomials for arbitrary system of nodes (1980) in Theorem 3.1 prove that for any node system $X$ there exists continuous $F$ such that $|L_n(F,X,x)|\to\infty$ for almost all $x\in[-1,1]$. Since $|L_n(F,X,x)| \le \|F\|_\infty L_n(x)$, this implies $\limsup\limits_{n\to\infty} L_n(x)=\infty$ for almost all $x\in[-1,1]$.
